item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(not sure if itcounts as necroposting if its just an old article)

did you actually burn the paper for page 6

item.title: Nice!
item.content:encoded: an SCP that makes you feel some emotion, I would consider it a good SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really much weight to this one, a bit too simplistic. More like a magic object from a D&D book than an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked the idea! Although I am kind of curious as to how 'Thomas' knows what's about to happen. Does he see a vague version of the event? Or does something just happen, like a flashback or a brief hallucination? It'd be interesting to ask that in an additional interview or something. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's rare to see a skip where Foundation researchers and the object contained willingly help each other out. Quite refreshing! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps the foundation could write the regular text in another language? That seems perfectly in character as the foundation would want to hide their own notes from the entity.

Of course, if the esteemed author doesn't know a 2nd language, he or she could just make one up and claim the foundation made it up just to make notes on this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very good. It was a clever gimmick, more so for its simplicity. I even wonder how the boy accessed the page he tried to use to make his escape part of the proceedings. And no, I don't want to see this on the article, it is good to have this detail out of the document, it is realistic to not write on the proceedings a detail that should be obvious to all people that mattered, and it should not be the in-universe reader's concern.

A solid upvote, and the right amount of information, no less, no more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good article +1
pencils

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only problem is that you had a redaction in the containment procedures. Other than that, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well hey, thanks!

Yeah, my original idea was that 3222-1 sees the 'final' version of each page it's given, so it already knows everything you're going to write, and basically fills in its responses. Because of my revision that doesn't really work anymore though, since the time was burned out of the page. Now, I guess, it can just straight-up see the near future, but it's still restricted because it has to communicate through the pencil.

And I do think there's potential for cool logs, sure— maybe it prevents some Keter-level breach, or maybe there could even be a log of the Foundation managing to free 3222-1 from its prison. That said, I do think I've told as much of the story of this object as I think I can; in particular, I don't think I'd be able to top my current ending in terms of coolness, because, you know, burned paper. So maybe eventually I'll add to the story, but for the moment I don't plan on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the burn marks and tainted paper gave a better sense of urgency and improved the article quality, I liked the idea of how SCP-3222-1 couldn't see parts of the paper after the fact. It gave more insight into its telepathic properties.

I don't know if this goes against your intent to make the ending ambiguous, but I'd love to see more content. To me, the last page - both in this revision and the first one - gave some potential for excellent logs especially given the unique hand-written element of SCP-3222.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, it should, nice catch! Fixed.

(Also, at first, I was seriously afraid that I had actually handwritten that mistake and would have to fix that, which would be a nightmare lol)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edit: Basically all that's in here is my me and my pencil.

Should it be me and my pencil?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My new three favorite words on mainlist are "I was startled."

I enjoy any SCP that uses new mediums and any SCP that allows the voice of a realistic Foundation author to come through.

You are an incredibly promising new author. Two first works of this caliber in such quick succession rarely happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are already prominent links, left-justified to the side, in red type, underneath each image, that clearly say "Page X transcript" :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although I understand that the pencil on paper method of transcription is a large part of this SCP, I think there should be a link to some sort of typed transcription of the article, for people who cannot read the handwriting.

EDIT: Or at least a copy of the writing in a non-cursive style.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Based on some consistent feedback I have changed the ending significantly. I think this version is much better, and I hope you do as well!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you're referring to the purple on the note on page 3, it's just shadows from scanning a crumpled piece of paper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the purple stuff?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, this is basically "how to justify writing an SCP in cursive on lined notebook paper, the SCP" but it kind of works. If nothing else, kids stuck in objects are always a pretty good idea.

item.title: Re: A question
item.content:encoded:
I wrote it all out with blanks where I later wrote 3222! I didn't fill them in until I was ready to pick a number, and naturally I did it as fast as I could.

it's actually decently noticeable, since the the weight and shape of the numbers doesn't quite match that of the rest of the document. Also, there are some iterations where the space left blank initially doesn't quite fit all the numbers consistently. But then, I'm a psyc major who has a few books on handwriting anaylsis

item.title: Re: A question
item.content:encoded: Smart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are you complaining about the loose leaf type lined paper or lined paper in general?

item.title: Re: A question
item.content:encoded:
I wrote it all out with blanks where I later wrote 3222! I didn't fill them in until I was ready to pick a number, and naturally I did it as fast as I could. (It sure would have been inconvenient to erase all of that…)

Also, it is written in pencil, I just darkened the scans so that it was more legible.

item.title: A question
item.content:encoded:
You had to write all this out, right? Well, what if 3222 was already taken?

Also, small nitpick, but the papers are written in pen and not pencil, at least, it looks like it's written in pen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good SCP there! +1 for me.

I love the concept of an SCP that can inhibit writing and I do believe this has alot of potential as becoming Thauminel in the future, although currently I can see why others have the problem with the ending.

The SCP-XXXX idea is a little harsh, considering it means we don't know exactly what SCP it was used against, and thus forth, what it can be used against. I like the concept, though, great work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hmm, i, for a lack of a better word, like? this article, i mean, this article gets a thumb up from me, but the article definitely needs work, but the thing that really got my upvote was the torn pieces of pages in place of data lost, that is, if that was the effect that you were going for

edit: mixed up redacted with data lost, for example: but the thing that really got my upvote was the torn pieces of pages in place of redacted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the format gimmick, I really do, but the actual article had trouble capturing my attention. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree that it may seem a bit extraneous, but it goes to show that the scip may actually be effective in handling breaches. My only qualm is that it happens too suddenly. It's just a casual "Hey, you're gonna die" and bam. Why didn't he just tell her directly? Minor qualm. Overall, I like the character and the effect, I just hope you smooth out the end addendum of you decide to keep it. Of course, editing will be a pain, but hopefully you smooth it out a bit. Up oat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending was unneeded, aside from that is article was grade A, with a quite lovely twist on classic formating. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, requiring the standard lined paper is part of the scp, so I don't have a problem.

I kinda liked it. Only thing that stopped me from an upvote, is how quick and jarring the last paragraph is.

"Oh, this other scp was nearby, came in and ate the researcher. Yep, that's it! Seeya later."

So yah, I'd suggest working on the tone and depth of the ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you had transcripts, I can't read cursive. Anyway, have a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point! Done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Keep in mind, especially for readers and authors who are at work/school, most photo sharing sites are blocked. You should probably upload the files directly to the wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can appreciate the bizarre approach this article takes, but cursive might not be the way to go for something like this. That's just my opinion, but it seems out of the norm of what the Foundation might do with official documents. Also I agree with MrAnakinSpecter about the lined paper.

I'm going to +1 this, but revisions are definitely needed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I do enjoy the actual paperwork, there are a few things things keeping me from giving this a +1. There are a few SCP-XXXX's in the article, and the standard lined paper seems a little unprofessional for an organization like the Foundation, in my opinion. This is a good concept that could have been executed cleaner. No vote.
